content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcES C oN,rMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 9-581.1-1329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-08tt6
August 6, 2008
Liane M. Randolph
Pill.bury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP
-100 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Sacramento, CA 958 14-4419
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-ll8
Dear Ms. Randolph
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the behested payment
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' We base the letter on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a
firrderof fact when itrenders advice. (ln re Oglesbv (1975) I FPPC Ops.7l.)
Additionally, ollr advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act.
QUESTTONS
l. If yor"rr client participatbs in a series of tax events to assist lower-income tax-filers and
notifies local elected officers of the events so that they might attend, will the cost of the
event be a reportable "behested payment" to the officers who attend the events?
2. lf the local elected officers include a statement in the press release about the events,
will it constitute a "behested payment" to these officers?
CONCLUSION
l. There is no contribution and no "behested payment" based on these facts
2. If the local elected officer includes a statemerlt and his or her name in the press
release, it will constitute a "behested payment" under the Act, and the cost of producing
and sending the press release r.l'ould be reporlable if $5,000 or more.
rThePolitical RefirrmActiscontainedinGorernmentCodc-Sectionsl{l(XX)throughgl0l+. All
statutor) ret'erenees arr'to the Government Cocle. unless otherwise inclicated. The re-culations rlf the Fair
Politieal PlacticesCommissionarecontainedinSeetirrnsItlII0throughI1t997ofTitle2oftheCalitbrnia
('odeofRegulations.,\ll re'gulatorylelerencesarctuTitle2.Dirision6oftheCalitirrniaCocleof
Regulutions. unless otherwise inclicated.


File No. A-08-l l8
Page No.2
FACTS
You represent Intuit, Inc., a software company that off'ers the "Turbo Tax"
software package to assist people in filing their state and federal tax retllrns. Intuit has a
foundation called the Intuit Financial Freedom Foundation ("Intuit"). Intuit wo,ld like to
join with the Computer and Communications Indr.rstry Association ("CCIA") and a local
community center to host a series of tax events to help lower income taxpayers prepare
and file their federal and state tax returns online at nocost to the to*poy..r.
The local community center will provide the facilities, use of their computers and
Internet services and will promote the events to their students. Intllit will donaie copies
of the Turbo Tax software, and its employees will volunteer at the events. CCIA will
promote the events in the media, including printing and distributing fliers and posters,
will provide translators for non-English speakers, and provide a $S,OOO honorarium to the
community center for hosting the events.
CCIA and lntuit would like to notify local elected officers of the contemplated
event dates and times should they wish to attend any of the events. They would also like
to offer the local elected officers an opportunity to include a statement in the press
releases to raise awareness about the events. You now ask whether this involvement by
local elected officers would constitute "behested payments" that wor-rld be reportable by
the elected officers under Section 82015(bX2)(iii).
ANALYSIS
The Act states"that. generally, a "contribution" includes a payment made at the
behest of a candidate.' lSection 820r5(b).) A payment is made oi tt " 
,,behest,' of a
candidate whenever it is made "under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the
express, prior consent ofl'a candidate. (Section 82015; Regulaiion 18225.7.) The term
"payment" includes an expenditure of money (see Sectio n 8204+1, and an expenditure
made at the behest of a candidate includes "expenditures made by a person oiher than the
candidate or committee, to fund a communication relating to" a "ondidot. who is clearly
identified. (Regularion t8225.7 (b).)
Section 82015(bX2), sets forth several exceptions to the general rule that a
payment made at the behest of a candidate is a contribution. Specifically, subparagraphs
(A) and (B) of Section 82015(b)(2) provide rhar rhe behested poyrnrnt is not a
contribution under the following circumstances:
"(A) Full and adeqtiate consideration is received from the candidate.
. I For ptlrposes of the Act. an elected ot'fer retains his or her status as a candidate until he or she
terminates that stattls as provide'd in Section 842 l4 and applicable Commission regulations. (Section
til(x)7. )


File No. A-08-l l8
Page No.3
("B) It is clear fiom the sumotmding circumstances that the payment
was made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective
office. The following types of payments are presLlmed to be for
purposes unrelated to a candidate's candidacy for elective office:
"(i) A payment made principally for personal purposes, in
which case it may be considered a gift under the provisions of
Section 82028. Payments that are otherwise subject to the limits
of Section 86203 are presllmed to be principally for personal
pLrrposes.
"(ii) A payment made by a state, local, or federal govemmental
agency or by a nonprofit organization that is exempt from
taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
"(iii) A payment not covered by clause (i), made principally for
legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes, in which case
it is neither a gift nor a contribution . . .."
section 82015(bX2XB)(iii), however, provides that, even if a payment made at
the behest of an elected officer is not a contribution to that officer, the oificer must file a
report detailing the payment if the payment is principally for a legislative, governmental
or charitable purpose. The report, known informally as a "behested payment report,,'
must be filed within 30 days with the officer's agency when the amount of the poyrn.nt,
or aggregate payments for similar pLlrposes made at the behest of the officer by the same
source, is $5,000 or more in a calendar year.
Based on the information you provided, it appears that any payments made by
Intuit, CCIA, or the local community centers in connection with the described events are
not related to the candidacy of any elected officer (see Secrion 82015(b)(2)(C), which
details actions that are "related to a candidate's candidacy") and would more
appropriately be classified as relating to and being principally for a govemmental or
charitable purpose. Therefore, these payments are not contributionJto the elected
officers who participate in these events, but may be reportable "behested payments',
under Section 82015(bX2XBXiii). We next address whether or not these payments are
reportable "behested payments."
You have requested advice based on two scenarios. In the first, CCIA and Intuit
would notify local elected officers of the contemplated event dates and times should they
wish to attend the events. Based on the definition of "behested payment," above, there is


File No. A-08-118
Page No.4
no behested payment in this situation, but rather an invitation to an event not "hosted" or
"sponsored" by the elected officer that the officer may or may not accept.'l
In the second scenario, CCIA and Intuit would offer the local elected officer an
opportunity to include a statement in the CCIA press releases to raise awareness about the
events. The officer would make the statement in coordination with CCIA, and CCIA
would make the expenditure to produce and send a communication related to an
identified candidate. Thus, as described above, under the Act and Commission
regulations, the payment in this second scenario is a behested payment, and if the cost to
produce and send it is $5,000 or more each elected officer who makes a statement in the
press release would be required to file a "behested payment" report. Additionally, if the
elected officer's statement in the press release suggests that he or she is "hosting" or
"sponsoring" the applicable event, the officer would report the cost of the event as a
whole, rather than only the cost of the producing and sending the press release.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9t6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
M. Rowan
fu{-
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
t Be aware. however. that it the otflcial receives a personal benefit while at the event. other than
"intbrmational materials" (see Section 8202tt(b)( l; and Regulation lU9-542. | ). it may constitute a gift
under the Act. (Sce Section tt202tl.)
